answer_md:
# Heterogeneous worker pools: context-complete / stateless workers with no file or tool access

## Summary

None of the three named frameworks — CrewAI, AutoGen/AG2, LangGraph — document a named pattern
specifically for "a worker with zero file/tool access that must receive a fully self-contained
task packet." All three *support* building such a worker (an agent/node with an empty tool list,
fed only inlined text), but the decision of what to inline vs. leave for the worker to fetch is
left entirely to the orchestrating developer's judgment — none of them formalize it as a schema or
named concept. The closest primary-source articulation of the *problem* (not a schema, but the
underlying design principle) is Anthropic's own "How we built our multi-agent research system"
engineering post, which is directly about a lead agent curating self-contained task descriptions
for subagents and is worth treating as a fourth data point even though it isn't a general-purpose
orchestration framework.

---

## CrewAI

Docs: [Tasks](https://docs.crewai.com/en/concepts/tasks), [Agents](https://docs.crewai.com/en/concepts/agents)

1. **Documented pattern/term for a stateless/context-complete worker?** No. CrewAI does not use
   the terms "stateless" or "context-complete" anywhere in the Agents/Tasks docs. It does support
   an agent with no tools (`tools` is optional, "Defaults to an empty list") — but this is framed
   as a normal configuration option, not a named worker archetype. There is a `reasoning=True` flag
   that makes an agent "reflect and create plans before executing," which is reasoning-only in a
   loose sense, but it's orthogonal to tool access and undocumented as a "context-complete" concept.

2. **How does the orchestrator decide what to inline vs. leave for the worker to fetch?** CrewAI's
   `Crew`/`Task` model has one explicit mechanism: the `context` attribute on a `Task`, which is a
   list of *other tasks* whose outputs get relayed forward — "Tasks can depend on the output of
   other tasks using the `context` attribute" — plus `role`/`goal`/`backstory` string fields that
   get template-interpolated into the agent's system prompt (`{role}`, `{goal}`, `{backstory}`).
   There is no documented decision procedure or guidance for *what* to put in `context` vs. leaving
   an agent to use a `tools` call instead — that judgment call is left entirely to the developer
   writing the Task/Agent definitions. No inlining heuristic is documented.

3. **Standard schema/shape for such a payload?** Partially. The `Task` object has a stable, named
   shape: `description`, `expected_output`, `context` (list of upstream Task outputs),
   `tools` (per-task tool override), `agent`, and `output_json`/`output_pydantic` for structured
   output. This is a real schema, but it's a general task-definition schema, not one purpose-built
   for a no-tool/no-file worker — there's no separate "payload" type for context-complete workers.

---

## AutoGen / AG2

Docs: [Context Variables](https://docs.ag2.ai/latest/docs/user-guide/advanced-concepts/orchestration/group-chat/context-variables/)

1. **Documented pattern/term for a stateless/context-complete worker?** No — and AG2 explicitly
   goes the *other* direction from "auto-inline everything." The docs state plainly: **"Context
   variables are not automatically included in the prompts sent to LLMs."** AG2's `ContextVariables`
   is a shared dict-like store (`ContextVariables(data={...})`, accessed via `.get()`/`.set()`/
   bracket notation) that survives across a group chat, but by design it is *not* a "here is your
   complete context, go reason" packet — it's closer to a session-scoped key-value store that
   individual agents must actively pull from.

2. **How does the orchestrating layer decide what context to inline vs. leave for the worker to
   fetch?** AG2 documents three explicit access methods rather than an automatic decision: (a) tools
   that receive `ContextVariables` via dependency injection, (b) `UpdateSystemMessage` templates
   that manually interpolate placeholders like `{user_name}` into the system prompt, and (c)
   dedicated "context summary" tools an agent calls to fetch state. The docs are explicit that this
   is a deliberate design choice, not an oversight: **"Developers have full control over what
   context the LLM sees and when."** There is no documented heuristic for the inline-vs-fetch
   decision — it's 100% manual per the framework's own philosophy.

3. **Standard schema/shape for such a payload?** No dedicated schema for a context-complete worker.
   `ContextVariables` itself has a shape (a flat dict wrapped in a class with typed accessors), but
   it is a shared-state container for a multi-turn conversation, not a self-contained
   one-shot task payload for a tool-less worker.

---

## LangGraph

Docs: [Subgraphs](https://docs.langchain.com/oss/python/langgraph/use-subgraphs),
[Context overview](https://docs.langchain.com/oss/python/concepts/context),
[`Pregel.context_schema` reference](https://reference.langchain.com/python/langgraph/pregel/main/Pregel/context_schema)

1. **Documented pattern/term for a stateless/context-complete worker?** No explicit term, but
   LangGraph comes closest to a *formal* mechanism among the three. It distinguishes **runtime
   context** (`context_schema`, immutable, injected once at invocation — "data and dependencies
   your code needs to run") from **state** (`state_schema`, mutable, evolves turn-to-turn). A
   subgraph node is also documented as operating as a "reusable, black-box node with its own
   private state" whose input is explicitly transformed before invocation — i.e., the parent node
   function does `subgraph.invoke({"bar": state["foo"]})`, hand-mapping exactly what the subgraph
   receives. This is architecturally what a "context-complete worker" would use, but LangGraph's
   own docs never name it as such, and there is no documented no-tool-access restriction tied to it
   — a subgraph can still have tools; statelessness here refers to persistence/checkpointing
   (`checkpointer=False` → "runs like a plain function call"), not to file/tool access.

2. **How does the orchestrating layer decide what context to inline vs. leave for the worker to
   fetch?** Not documented as a decision procedure. The parent node's transform function is where a
   developer manually maps parent state onto subgraph input — LangGraph provides the mechanism
   (`context_schema` + input/output transform functions at the subgraph boundary) but no guidance
   on *what* should go in state vs. context vs. be left for a tool call.

3. **Standard schema/shape for such a payload?** Closest of the three to "yes." `context_schema` is
   a first-class, typed construct: define a `@dataclass class ContextSchema: field: type`, and
   nodes/tools access it as `runtime.context.field` via the generic `Runtime[ContextT]` class,
   passed at `invoke(context=ContextSchema(...))`. This is a real, named, typed schema boundary —
   but it's designed for *dependency injection* (DB handles, user IDs, API clients), not
   specifically for "paste all the text a tool-less reasoning-only worker needs." Nothing in the
   docs restricts `context_schema` payloads to plain text/no-tool workers.

---

## Bonus: Anthropic's own multi-agent research system (not one of the 3 named, but directly on-point)

Source: [How we built our multi-agent research system](https://www.anthropic.com/engineering/multi-agent-research-system)

This is the most relevant primary source found, because it describes exactly David's "smart-agent
curates for a less-capable/less-equipped agent" scenario, using Claude agents rather than a
framework. It documents (again, no formal named pattern, but explicit prescriptive guidance):

- The lead agent must give each subagent **"an objective, an output format, guidance on the tools
  and sources to use, and clear task boundaries."**
- Under-specification is called out as the concrete failure mode this guards against: vague
  instructions (e.g. "research the semiconductor shortage") caused subagents to **"misinterpret the
  task or perform the exact same searches as other agents,"** i.e., duplicated work and gaps.
- No named schema is given for the task description — it's prose guidance on *what fields a good
  task description covers* (objective / output format / tool-and-source guidance / boundaries),
  not a JSON/typed schema. There's also no explicit "how much to inline vs. let the subagent go
  find out" decision rule — the fix Anthropic reports is simply "make the lead write a more
  detailed task description," which is a heuristic, not a formalized inline/fetch boundary.

---

## Relevance to dark-factory

Dark-factory's `external-research` queue convention (`engine/store/queue/.CONVENTION.md`) already
independently arrived at the closest real-world analogue to what all four sources gesture at but
none of them formalize: a ticket JSON with `question` + `context` (context is *always* inlined
literal text, since "the consumer can't read files") + an `output_schema` the consumer must fill in.
That is a genuine "context-complete worker payload" schema — arguably more concrete than anything
found in CrewAI's `Task`, AG2's `ContextVariables`, or LangGraph's `context_schema`, none of which
ship a schema tailored to a *zero-tool, zero-file, single-shot* consumer.

What this research validates: the shape (question + inlined context + explicit output schema) is
sound and matches the *spirit* of Anthropic's own advice — objective, output format, and clear
boundaries — even though Anthropic's subagents still have tool access and dark-factory's don't.
What this research suggests as an improvement: none of the frameworks, including Anthropic's,
document a decision *procedure* for what to inline — it's uniformly "more detail is safer, ambiguity
causes duplicated work or gaps." Dark-factory's ticket-writer (the orchestrator) should treat that as
license to err on the side of over-inlining context in `context` fields rather than trying to derive
a principled minimal-context rule — no framework surveyed offers one. The one concrete upgrade worth
borrowing from Anthropic's writeup: explicitly separating "tool/source guidance" from "task
boundaries" as distinct fields in the task description was called out as what fixed their duplication
problem — dark-factory's `context` field currently blends question framing and background context
together; a future revision could consider splitting "boundaries / scope" out as its own labeled
field the way Anthropic's lead-agent prompts do, to reduce the risk of an external agent going out of
scope or duplicating other tickets' work.
notes: No framework surveyed (CrewAI, AutoGen/AG2, LangGraph) documents a named pattern for a worker with zero file/tool access requiring a fully self-contained payload; all support building one (empty tool list + inlined text) but treat the inline-vs-fetch decision as an undocumented developer judgment call. LangGraph's context_schema/Runtime[ContextT] is the closest to a formal typed schema, but it's a dependency-injection mechanism, not a no-tool-worker-specific construct. Added Anthropic's own multi-agent research system engineering post as a 4th, highly relevant primary source since it directly addresses 'lead agent curates self-contained task description for a subagent' even though it isn't a general orchestration framework and its subagents do retain tool access. Confidence: medium -- absence-of-pattern claims are well-supported by official docs, but the survey did not exhaustively read every AG2/LangGraph page (e.g. AG2's GroupChat / Swarm docs, LangGraph's Command/handoff docs) so a narrower doc corner naming this explicitly cannot be fully ruled out.
